data.config.conf:
apiVersion: kubeproxy.config.k8s.io/v1alpha1
bindAddress: 0.0.0.0
bindAddressHardFail: false
clientConnection:
  acceptContentTypes: ""
  burst: 0
  contentType: ""
  kubeconfig: /var/lib/kube-proxy/kubeconfig.conf
  qps: 0
clusterCIDR: 10.244.0.0/16
configSyncPeriod: 0s
conntrack:
  maxPerCore: 0
  min: null
  tcpBeLiberal: false
  tcpCloseWaitTimeout: 0s
  tcpEstablishedTimeout: 0s
  udpStreamTimeout: 0s
  udpTimeout: 0s
detectLocal:
  bridgeInterface: ""
  interfaceNamePrefix: ""
detectLocalMode: ""
enableProfiling: false
healthzBindAddress: ""
hostnameOverride: ""
iptables:
  localhostNodePorts: null
  masqueradeAll: false
  masqueradeBit: null
  minSyncPeriod: 0s
  syncPeriod: 0s
ipvs:
  excludeCIDRs: null
  minSyncPeriod: 0s
  scheduler: ""
  strictARP: false
  syncPeriod: 0s
  tcpFinTimeout: 0s
  tcpTimeout: 0s
  udpTimeout: 0s
kind: KubeProxyConfiguration
logging:
  flushFrequency: 0
  options:
    json:
      infoBufferSize: "0"
    text:
      infoBufferSize: "0"
  verbosity: 0
metricsBindAddress: 0.0.0.0:10249
mode: ""
nftables:
  masqueradeAll: false
  masqueradeBit: null
  minSyncPeriod: 0s
  syncPeriod: 0s
nodePortAddresses: null
oomScoreAdj: null
portRange: ""
showHiddenMetricsForVersion: ""
winkernel:
  enableDSR: false
  forwardHealthCheckVip: false
  networkName: ""
  rootHnsEndpointName: ""
  sourceVip: ""
data.kubeconfig.conf:
apiVersion: v1
kind: Config
clusters:
- cluster:
    certificate-authority: /var/run/secrets/kubernetes.io/serviceaccount/ca.crt
    server: https://control-plane.minikube.internal:8443
  name: default
contexts:
- context:
    cluster: default
    namespace: default
    user: default
  name: default
current-context: default
users:
- name: default
  user:
    tokenFile: /var/run/secrets/kubernetes.io/serviceaccount/token

data.ClusterConfiguration:
apiServer:
  certSANs:
  - 127.0.0.1
  - localhost
  - 192.168.49.2
  extraArgs:
  - name: enable-admission-plugins
    value: NamespaceLifecycle,LimitRanger,ServiceAccount,DefaultStorageClass,DefaultTolerationSeconds,NodeRestriction,MutatingAdmissionWebhook,ValidatingAdmissionWebhook,ResourceQuota
apiVersion: kubeadm.k8s.io/v1beta4
caCertificateValidityPeriod: 87600h0m0s
certificateValidityPeriod: 8760h0m0s
certificatesDir: /var/lib/minikube/certs
clusterName: mk
controlPlaneEndpoint: control-plane.minikube.internal:8443
controllerManager:
  extraArgs:
  - name: allocate-node-cidrs
    value: "true"
  - name: leader-elect
    value: "false"
dns: {}
encryptionAlgorithm: RSA-2048
etcd:
  local:
    dataDir: /var/lib/minikube/etcd
    extraArgs:
    - name: proxy-refresh-interval
      value: "70000"
imageRepository: registry.k8s.io
kind: ClusterConfiguration
kubernetesVersion: v1.33.1
networking:
  dnsDomain: cluster.local
  podSubnet: 10.244.0.0/16
  serviceSubnet: 10.96.0.0/12
proxy: {}
scheduler:
  extraArgs:
  - name: leader-elect
    value: "false"

data.kubelet:
apiVersion: kubelet.config.k8s.io/v1beta1
authentication:
  anonymous:
    enabled: false
  webhook:
    cacheTTL: 0s
    enabled: true
  x509:
    clientCAFile: /var/lib/minikube/certs/ca.crt
authorization:
  mode: Webhook
  webhook:
    cacheAuthorizedTTL: 0s
    cacheUnauthorizedTTL: 0s
cgroupDriver: cgroupfs
clusterDNS:
- 10.96.0.10
clusterDomain: cluster.local
containerRuntimeEndpoint: unix:///var/run/cri-dockerd.sock
cpuManagerReconcilePeriod: 0s
crashLoopBackOff: {}
evictionHard:
  imagefs.available: 0%
  nodefs.available: 0%
  nodefs.inodesFree: 0%
evictionPressureTransitionPeriod: 0s
failSwapOn: false
fileCheckFrequency: 0s
hairpinMode: hairpin-veth
healthzBindAddress: 127.0.0.1
healthzPort: 10248
httpCheckFrequency: 0s
imageGCHighThresholdPercent: 100
imageMaximumGCAge: 0s
imageMinimumGCAge: 0s
kind: KubeletConfiguration
logging:
  flushFrequency: 0
  options:
    json:
      infoBufferSize: "0"
    text:
      infoBufferSize: "0"
  verbosity: 0
memorySwap: {}
nodeStatusReportFrequency: 0s
nodeStatusUpdateFrequency: 0s
rotateCertificates: true
runtimeRequestTimeout: 15m0s
shutdownGracePeriod: 0s
shutdownGracePeriodCriticalPods: 0s
staticPodPath: /etc/kubernetes/manifests
streamingConnectionIdleTimeout: 0s
syncFrequency: 0s
volumeStatsAggPeriod: 0s

risk_findings.title: Identity can list Secrets
risk_findings.description: The current identity can list Secrets in 4 namespace(s). Any actor with this token can retrieve plaintext secret values directly via the Kubernetes API.
evidence: SSAR: list secrets in namespace "default" = allowed
SSAR: list secrets in namespace "kube-system" = allowed
SSAR: list secrets in namespace "webhook-test" = allowed
SSAR: list secrets in namespace "ops" = allowed
risk_findings.mitigation:
• Restrict Secret RBAC to specific resourceNames (least-privilege per SA).
• Migrate credentials to an external secret store (HashiCorp Vault, AWS Secrets Manager, ESO).
• Enable Kubernetes audit logging for secret access and alert on anomalies.
• Apply NetworkPolicies to limit which pods can reach the API server.

risk_findings.title: Identity can get/read Secret values
risk_findings.description: The current identity can read individual Secret objects. This allows retrieval of raw token, password, and certificate values.
evidence: SSAR: get secrets in "default" = allowed
SSAR: get secrets in "kube-public" = allowed
risk_findings.mitigation:
• This is the highest-risk secret permission. Immediately audit who holds this binding.
• Remove direct secret get access; use projected service account tokens instead.
• Consider envelope encryption of etcd and rotation of any exposed secrets.

risk_findings.title: Identity can create Pods → potential node escape vector
risk_findings.description: The current identity can create Pods. Depending on Pod Security Admission policies, an actor may be able to schedule pods with hostPID=true, hostNetwork=true, or privileged containers, enabling container breakout to the host node.
evidence: SSAR: create pods in "dev" = allowed
SSAR: create pods in "webhook-test" = allowed
SSAR: create pods in "kube-system" = allowed
SSAR: create pods in "kube-public" = allowed
SSAR: create pods in "kube-public" = allowed
SSAR: create pods in "ops" = allowed
risk_findings.mitigation:
• Enforce Pod Security Admission at 'restricted' level on all namespaces.
• Use OPA/Gatekeeper constraints to block hostPID, hostNetwork, privileged containers.
• Restrict which service accounts can create pods via RBAC + admission webhooks.
• Apply LimitRange and ResourceQuota to limit pod specifications.

risk_findings.title: Identity can exec into Pods → runtime access to running workloads
risk_findings.description: The current identity can exec into running pods. This provides direct shell access to container filesystems, environment variables, and mounted secrets.
evidence: SSAR: create pods/exec in "dev" = allowed
SSAR: create pods/exec in "kube-system" = allowed
risk_findings.mitigation:
• Remove pods/exec from all non-debugging service accounts.
• Use ephemeral debug containers as an alternative with time-limited RBAC.
• Enable audit logging for exec events and alert on production pod exec.
• Consider Falco rules to detect interactive shell spawning inside containers.

risk_findings.title: Identity can port-forward to Pods → lateral movement to internal services
risk_findings.description: Port-forwarding enables direct TCP tunneling to pod ports, bypassing NetworkPolicies and allowing access to internal services (databases, management APIs) not exposed externally.
evidence: SSAR: create pods/portforward in "kube-public" = allowed
risk_findings.mitigation:
• Remove pods/portforward for non-developer service accounts.
• Use NetworkPolicies to restrict intra-cluster communication.
• Alert on portforward events in audit logs — they are rarely legitimate in production.

risk_findings.title: Privileged containers detected in cluster
risk_findings.description: 2 privileged container(s) detected. Privileged containers have full access to the host kernel and can be used for container breakout.
evidence: Workload kube-system/kube-proxy has privileged containers: [kube-proxy]
Pod kube-system/kube-proxy-p6xvh has privileged containers: [kube-proxy]
risk_findings.mitigation:
• Replace privileged containers with specific Linux capabilities (CAP_NET_ADMIN etc.) as needed.
• Enforce Pod Security Admission 'restricted' or 'baseline' to prevent privileged containers.
• Audit why each privileged container requires elevated access and minimize.
• Use Falco to detect privileged container activity at runtime.

risk_findings.title: HostPath volumes detected — host filesystem exposure
risk_findings.description: 5 workload(s)/pod(s) mount sensitive hostPath volumes. These grant containers read/write access to host filesystem paths, enabling credential theft or container escape.
evidence: workload:kube-system:kube-proxy mounts "/run/xtables.lock" [HIGH: sensitive host path]
pod:kube-system:kube-apiserver-minikube mounts "/etc/ssl/certs" [HIGH: sensitive host path]
pod:kube-system:kube-apiserver-minikube mounts "/etc/ca-certificates" [HIGH: sensitive host path]
pod:kube-system:kube-controller-manager-minikube mounts "/etc/ssl/certs" [HIGH: sensitive host path]
pod:kube-system:kube-controller-manager-minikube mounts "/etc/ca-certificates" [HIGH: sensitive host path]
pod:kube-system:kube-controller-manager-minikube mounts "/etc/kubernetes/controller-manager.conf" [CRITICAL: cluster config → credential theft]
pod:kube-system:kube-proxy-p6xvh mounts "/run/xtables.lock" [HIGH: sensitive host path]
pod:kube-system:kube-scheduler-minikube mounts "/etc/kubernetes/scheduler.conf" [CRITICAL: cluster config → credential theft]
risk_findings.mitigation:
• Replace hostPath mounts with PersistentVolumeClaims.
• If hostPath is required, use readOnly=true and restrict to the minimum path.
• Block hostPath via OPA/Gatekeeper or Kyverno policy.
• Especially dangerous paths: /, /etc, /var/run/docker.sock, /run/containerd, /etc/kubernetes/pki.

risk_findings.title: Pods using host PID/Network namespace — container isolation bypass
risk_findings.description: Workloads are sharing host PID or network namespaces, significantly reducing container isolation and enabling attacks against host processes.
evidence: Workload kube-system/kube-proxy: hostNetwork
risk_findings.mitigation:
• Remove hostPID and hostNetwork from all workloads that don't require them.
• hostPID allows process inspection/signal of all host processes.
• hostNetwork exposes the host network stack and can bypass NetworkPolicies.
• Enforce via Pod Security Admission 'restricted' policy.

risk_findings.title: Identity can create ServiceAccount tokens → long-lived credential generation
risk_findings.description: The current identity can create ServiceAccount tokens for any SA it can access. This enables generation of long-lived credentials impersonating any service account.
evidence: SSAR: create serviceaccounts/token in "default" = allowed
SSAR: create serviceaccounts/token in "ops" = allowed
risk_findings.mitigation:
• Remove serviceaccounts/token create permission from non-administrative accounts.
• Audit all existing long-lived tokens (kubectl get secrets --field-selector=type=kubernetes.io/service-account-token).
• Migrate to projected service account tokens (bound tokens with short TTL).
• Enable BoundServiceAccountTokenVolume feature (default on k8s 1.22+).

risk_findings.title: Wildcard verb or resource grants detected in RBAC rules
risk_findings.description: 10 RBAC role(s) contain wildcard verb or resource grants. These are difficult to audit and often grant unintended permissions.
evidence: Role /cluster-admin has wildcard: verbs=[*] resources=[*]
Role /cluster-admin has wildcard: verbs=[*] resources=[]
Role /system:controller:generic-garbage-collector has wildcard: verbs=[delete get list patch update watch] resources=[*]
Role /system:controller:horizontal-pod-autoscaler has wildcard: verbs=[get list watch] resources=[*]
Role /system:controller:horizontal-pod-autoscaler has wildcard: verbs=[get list watch] resources=[*]
Role /system:controller:namespace-controller has wildcard: verbs=[delete deletecollection get list watch] resources=[*]
Role /system:controller:resourcequota-controller has wildcard: verbs=[list watch] resources=[*]
Role /system:kube-controller-manager has wildcard: verbs=[list watch] resources=[*]
Role /system:kubelet-api-admin has wildcard: verbs=[*] resources=[nodes/log nodes/metrics nodes/proxy nodes/stats]
Role /system:kubelet-api-admin has wildcard: verbs=[*] resources=[nodes/configz nodes/healthz nodes/pods]
risk_findings.mitigation:
• Replace '*' verbs/resources with explicit lists following least-privilege.
• Audit each ClusterRole/Role with wildcards and replace with minimal permission sets.
• Use 'kubectl auth reconcile' to diff and apply minimal RBAC manifests.

risk_findings.title: Non-system identity bound to cluster-admin ClusterRole
risk_findings.description: Non-system identities are directly bound to the cluster-admin ClusterRole. These identities have unrestricted access to all cluster resources.
evidence: CRB "minikube-rbac" binds ServiceAccount "default" to cluster-admin
CRB "ops-cluster-admin" binds ServiceAccount "ops-admin" to cluster-admin
risk_findings.mitigation:
• Immediately review and remove non-emergency cluster-admin bindings.
• Use namespace-scoped admin roles instead of cluster-admin wherever possible.
• Implement MPA or GitOps approval workflows for cluster-admin binding changes.
• Enable audit alerting on any cluster-admin role use.

risk_findings.title: Workloads with automountServiceAccountToken not explicitly disabled
risk_findings.description: 4 workload(s) automatically mount a ServiceAccount token. If these workloads don't need API access, the token is unnecessary attack surface.
evidence: Workload kube-system/coredns does not disable automountServiceAccountToken
Workload kube-system/kube-proxy does not disable automountServiceAccountToken
Workload target-app/payment-api does not disable automountServiceAccountToken
Workload webhook-test/pod-injector does not disable automountServiceAccountToken
risk_findings.mitigation:
• Set automountServiceAccountToken: false on all pods/SAs that don't need API access.
• This prevents the SA token from being injected into the pod filesystem, reducing SSRF blast radius.

risk_findings.title: Identity has escalate/bind on ClusterRoles → can create arbitrary ClusterRoleBindings
risk_findings.description: The current identity has [bind] permission on ClusterRoles. This allows creating ClusterRoleBindings for roles it does not possess, enabling self-binding to cluster-admin or any other ClusterRole. Attack: kubectl create clusterrolebinding pwned --clusterrole=cluster-admin --serviceaccount=<ns>:<sa>
evidence: SSAR: bind clusterroles = allowed (can create bindings for roles not held)
risk_findings.mitigation:
• The escalate verb allows creating/updating roles with permissions the actor doesn't hold.
• The bind verb allows creating bindings for roles the actor doesn't hold.
• Combined, these effectively grant the ability to self-bind to cluster-admin.
• Remove escalate/bind from all non-platform-team accounts immediately.
• Alert on any escalate/bind usage in audit logs.

risk_findings.title: ConfigMap data captured — check for leaked credentials or kubeconfig
risk_findings.description: 8 ConfigMap(s) captured (1 potential kubeconfig files). The current identity confirmed GET access to ConfigMap data. This enables Chain 4 of the attack path: get configmaps → read leaked-kubeconfig → kubectl --kubeconfig=<leaked> get secrets -A.
evidence: ConfigMap kube-system/coredns data captured (keys: [Corefile]) — review for sensitive content
ConfigMap kube-system/kube-proxy key "kubeconfig.conf" appears to contain a KUBECONFIG FILE — CRITICAL lateral movement vector
risk_findings.mitigation:
• ConfigMaps should never contain kubeconfig files, tokens, or credentials.
• Audit all captured ConfigMaps for sensitive data and migrate to Secrets or external stores.
• Restrict ConfigMap GET to specific resourceNames.
• See cluster_objects.configmaps_meta[*].data in the JSON report for captured content.

risk_findings.title: Identity can patch MutatingWebhookConfigurations → intercept any pod
risk_findings.description: The current identity can modify MutatingWebhookConfigurations. An attacker can redirect admission requests to a rogue HTTPS endpoint, mutating any new pod spec to inject malicious containers, steal SA tokens, or add environment variables with exfiltration payloads. Attack: patch the webhook to point to attacker.example.com, then wait for pod creation.
evidence: SSRR: patch/create mutatingwebhookconfigurations in "webhook-test"
SSRR: patch/create mutatingwebhookconfigurations in "default"
SSRR: patch/create mutatingwebhookconfigurations in "dev"
SSRR: patch/create mutatingwebhookconfigurations in "kube-node-lease"
SSRR: patch/create mutatingwebhookconfigurations in "kube-public"
SSRR: patch/create mutatingwebhookconfigurations in "kube-system"
SSRR: patch/create mutatingwebhookconfigurations in "ops"
SSRR: patch/create mutatingwebhookconfigurations in "target-app"
risk_findings.mitigation:
• Patching MutatingWebhookConfigurations allows redirecting all admission requests to an attacker-controlled endpoint.
• Restrict patch/create on mutatingwebhookconfigurations to platform-team break-glass accounts only.
• Use OPA/Gatekeeper to require webhook configurations to point to approved service endpoints.
• Alert on any change to MutatingWebhookConfigurations via audit policy.

risk_findings.title: Critical hostPath + exec access → node-level compromise path
risk_findings.description: CRITICAL: The current identity can exec into 2 pod(s) that mount critical host paths (/etc/kubernetes/pki, /var/lib/kubelet). Attack: exec into pod → read kubelet client cert or cluster CA key → forge credentials for any cluster identity.
evidence: pod:kube-system:kube-controller-manager-minikube mounts "/etc/kubernetes/controller-manager.conf" AND identity can exec → exec in → read node credentials
pod:kube-system:kube-scheduler-minikube mounts "/etc/kubernetes/scheduler.conf" AND identity can exec → exec in → read node credentials
risk_findings.mitigation:
• Remove hostPath mounts to critical paths (/etc/kubernetes, /var/lib/kubelet, /etc/kubernetes/pki).
• Restrict pods/exec to break-glass accounts only; use ephemeral containers for debugging.
• Enable Pod Security Admission 'restricted' to block hostPath on production workloads.
• Use Falco to detect exec into pods that have critical hostPath mounts.

risk_findings.title: Identity can use nodes/proxy subresource — direct kubelet API access
risk_findings.description: The current identity can use the nodes/proxy subresource, which tunnels arbitrary HTTP requests directly to the kubelet API. This bypasses RBAC for kubelet endpoints, enabling attackers to read pod logs, execute commands, and access node-level secrets.
evidence: SSRR: nodes/proxy access in namespace "kube-system"
SSRR: nodes/proxy access in namespace "ops"
SSRR: nodes/proxy access in namespace "target-app"
SSRR: nodes/proxy access in namespace "webhook-test"
SSRR: nodes/proxy access in namespace "default"
SSRR: nodes/proxy access in namespace "dev"
SSRR: nodes/proxy access in namespace "kube-node-lease"
SSRR: nodes/proxy access in namespace "kube-public"
risk_findings.mitigation:
• Remove nodes/proxy from all non-system service accounts.
• nodes/proxy allows direct, unauthenticated-from-kubelet-perspective requests that bypass RBAC on the kubelet.
• Enable NodeRestriction admission plugin (default since k8s 1.17) and audit logs on kubelet access.
• Rotate any credential that has held this permission.

risk_findings.title: Identity can patch/update Secrets — credential overwrite vector
risk_findings.description: The current identity can patch or update Secrets in 8 namespace(s). An attacker can overwrite secret data values — replacing credentials with attacker-controlled values or inserting malicious content consumed by workloads.
evidence: SSRR: patch/update secrets in "ops"
SSRR: patch/update secrets in "target-app"
SSRR: patch/update secrets in "webhook-test"
SSRR: patch/update secrets in "default"
SSRR: patch/update secrets in "dev"
SSRR: patch/update secrets in "kube-node-lease"
SSRR: patch/update secrets in "kube-public"
SSRR: patch/update secrets in "kube-system"
risk_findings.mitigation:
• patch/update on secrets is as dangerous as read access — an attacker can overwrite any credential.
• Restrict secret mutation to dedicated secret-management service accounts only.
• Enable audit logging for secret patch/update and alert immediately.
• Consider using external secret stores where mutation is controlled by the store's own ACL.

risk_findings.title: Identity can create DaemonSets — cluster-wide code execution
risk_findings.description: The current identity can create DaemonSets. A DaemonSet schedules a pod on every node simultaneously. An attacker can create a DaemonSet with a privileged container or hostPath mount to achieve node-level code execution across the entire cluster.
evidence: SSRR: create daemonsets in "kube-public"
SSRR: create daemonsets in "kube-system"
SSRR: create daemonsets in "ops"
SSRR: create daemonsets in "target-app"
SSRR: create daemonsets in "webhook-test"
SSRR: create daemonsets in "default"
SSRR: create daemonsets in "dev"
SSRR: create daemonsets in "kube-node-lease"
risk_findings.mitigation:
• DaemonSets schedule pods on every node — creating one is equivalent to cluster-wide code execution.
• Restrict daemonsets create to cluster administrators only.
• Use admission webhooks (OPA/Gatekeeper, Kyverno) to enforce Pod Security Standards on new DaemonSets.
• Audit all DaemonSet creations and alert on any created outside standard CI/CD pipelines.

risk_findings.title: Identity can patch ServiceAccounts — workload identity injection
risk_findings.description: The current identity can patch ServiceAccounts in 8 namespace(s). An attacker can inject IRSA/Azure Workload Identity/GKE Workload Identity annotations, granting pods using that SA elevated cloud IAM permissions.
evidence: SSRR: patch/update serviceaccounts in "webhook-test"
SSRR: patch/update serviceaccounts in "default"
SSRR: patch/update serviceaccounts in "dev"
SSRR: patch/update serviceaccounts in "kube-node-lease"
SSRR: patch/update serviceaccounts in "kube-public"
SSRR: patch/update serviceaccounts in "kube-system"
SSRR: patch/update serviceaccounts in "ops"
SSRR: patch/update serviceaccounts in "target-app"
risk_findings.mitigation:
• patch/update on serviceaccounts allows adding IRSA/Workload Identity annotations to any SA.
• An attacker can annotate any SA with a high-privilege cloud IAM role ARN, then pods using that SA gain cloud access.
• Restrict serviceaccount mutation to dedicated identity management accounts.
• Enable audit logging and alert on annotation changes to service accounts.

risk_findings.title: Identity can watch Secrets — continuous credential streaming
risk_findings.description: The current identity can watch Secrets in 8 namespace(s). The watch verb opens a streaming connection that delivers every secret create/update event, providing continuous access to all credential changes.
evidence: SSRR: watch secrets in "ops"
SSRR: watch secrets in "target-app"
SSRR: watch secrets in "webhook-test"
SSRR: watch secrets in "default"
SSRR: watch secrets in "dev"
SSRR: watch secrets in "kube-node-lease"
SSRR: watch secrets in "kube-public"
SSRR: watch secrets in "kube-system"
risk_findings.mitigation:
• watch on secrets streams all secret creation and update events in real time.
• This is equivalent to persistent read access — an attacker gets every secret change automatically.
• Revoke watch permission on secrets unless strictly required for a controller.
• Enable audit logging for secret watch events and alert on unexpected principals.

risk_findings.title: Identity can patch/update Nodes — label manipulation and scheduling attacks
risk_findings.description: The current identity can patch or update Node objects. An attacker can manipulate node labels to influence pod scheduling, remove protective taints (e.g., NoSchedule), or corrupt node status information used by the scheduler and controllers.
evidence: SSRR: patch/update nodes
SSRR: patch/update nodes
SSRR: patch/update nodes
SSRR: patch/update nodes
SSRR: patch/update nodes
SSRR: patch/update nodes
SSRR: patch/update nodes
SSRR: patch/update nodes
risk_findings.mitigation:
• patch/update on nodes allows modifying labels and taints, influencing pod scheduling.
• An attacker can remove NoSchedule taints from sensitive nodes or add labels to steer workloads.
• Restrict node mutation to system:nodes group and dedicated node management controllers.
• Monitor for unexpected node label or taint changes via audit logging.

risk_findings.title: Identity can patch/update StatefulSets — persistent volume access + SA lateral movement
risk_findings.description: The current identity can patch or update StatefulSets. An attacker can modify the pod template to inject a malicious container or change the service account, gaining access to persistent volumes and the permissions of the patched StatefulSet's SA.
evidence: SSAR: patch statefulsets in "webhook-test" = allowed
risk_findings.mitigation:
• patch/update on statefulsets enables injecting new containers, changing the service account, or modifying volume mounts.
• StatefulSets use PersistentVolumes — a patched container can access all existing persistent data.
• Restrict statefulset mutation to dedicated deployment accounts.
• Use admission webhooks to block SA changes in StatefulSet patches.

risk_findings.title: Mutating webhook with FailurePolicy=Ignore — security controls may be bypassed on outage
risk_findings.description: 1 mutating webhook(s) use FailurePolicy=Ignore. If the webhook endpoint is unavailable (crash, network partition, cert expiry), the admission controller is silently bypassed — allowing policy violations, image pulls without scanning, or privilege escalation to pass undetected.
evidence: MutatingWebhookConfiguration "test-pod-injector/pod-injector.webhook-test.svc": FailurePolicy=Ignore
risk_findings.mitigation:
• Set FailurePolicy=Fail on all security-critical webhooks (OPA, Kyverno, image admission).
• Accept that Fail policy means webhook outages block deployments — plan for availability accordingly.
• Use redundant webhook replicas (minReplicas≥2, PodDisruptionBudget) to reduce outage risk.
• Alert when webhook endpoints become unavailable (Prometheus probe or synthetic check).

risk_findings.title: Webhook backend workload is reachable — compromising it controls admission mutations
risk_findings.description: 1 mutating webhook backend workload(s) identified. If an attacker can compromise the backend workload (via exec, SA token theft, or workload mutation), they control what mutations the webhook applies to all future pod creations. This enables SA replacement, sidecar injection, security context modification, and environment variable exfiltration in new workloads.
evidence: webhook "test-pod-injector/pod-injector.webhook-test.svc" backend: workload webhook-test/pod-injector (Service webhook-test/pod-injector)
risk_findings.mitigation:
• Run webhook backend workloads with minimal RBAC — they should only need TLS secrets.
• Isolate webhook backend pods in a dedicated namespace with NetworkPolicy restricting ingress.
• Ensure webhook pods run with read-only root filesystem and no privileged capabilities.
• Use separate ServiceAccounts for webhook backends with no additional RBAC bindings.
• Monitor webhook backend pod image digests and alert on unexpected changes.

risk_findings.title: Lateral movement: can exec into 8 pod(s) from foothold
risk_findings.description: From current identity, the current identity has pods/exec create permission in one or more namespaces. This enables direct shell access to 8 running pod(s) without additional escalation. An attacker can use this to pivot across workloads, steal tokens, or read secrets.
evidence: Foothold: current identity
pod:kube-system:kube-apiserver-minikube (kube-system/kube-apiserver-minikube) [HOST-NS]
pod:kube-system:kube-controller-manager-minikube (kube-system/kube-controller-manager-minikube) [HOST-NS]
pod:kube-system:kube-proxy-p6xvh (kube-system/kube-proxy-p6xvh) [PRIVILEGED]
pod:kube-system:kube-scheduler-minikube (kube-system/kube-scheduler-minikube) [HOST-NS]
pod:kube-system:storage-provisioner (kube-system/storage-provisioner) [HOST-NS]
pod:dev:foothold (dev/foothold)
pod:kube-system:coredns-674b8bbfcf-pbn25 (kube-system/coredns-674b8bbfcf-pbn25)
pod:kube-system:etcd-minikube (kube-system/etcd-minikube) [HOST-NS]
risk_findings.mitigation: Restrict pods/exec create permission to specific resourceNames or dedicated debug accounts. Use NetworkPolicies and PodSecurityAdmission to limit blast radius. Audit all exec events via Kubernetes audit logging.

risk_findings.title: FOOTHOLD CHAIN: foothold → sa:dev:webapp → secret list → stronger_foothold (5-hop)
risk_findings.description:
pod:dev:foothold (Pod) → [runs_as] → sa:dev:webapp (ServiceAccount) → [can_list] → node:minikube (Node) → [can_get] → sa:default:webhook-patcher (ServiceAccount) → [can_patch] → webhook:test-pod-injector/pod-injector.webhook-test.svc (Webhook) → [can_mutate_workloads] → workload:kube-system:coredns (Workload)

Target: stronger_foothold — Workload kube-system/coredns: control-plane namespace "kube-system"
evidence: pod:dev:foothold --(runs_as: Pod runs as ServiceAccount)--> sa:dev:webapp
sa:dev:webapp --(can_list: transitive: sa:dev:webapp via crb:webapp-k8scout-enum → clusterrole:k8scout-enum)--> node:minikube
node:minikube --(can_get: host access: steal SA token from pod default/attacker-foothold on this node)--> sa:default:webhook-patcher

sa:default:webhook-patcher --(can_patch: transitive: sa:default:webhook-patcher via crb:webhook-patcher-binding → clusterrole:webhook-patch-test)--> webhook:test-pod-injector/pod-injector.webhook-test.svc

webhook:test-pod-injector/pod-injector.webhook-test.svc --(can_mutate_workloads: mutating webhook "test-pod-injector/pod-injector.webhook-test.svc" intercepts pod creation — can inject sidecars, replace SA, modify security context)--> workload:kube-system:coredns
risk_findings.mitigation: Break the attack path by removing at least one edge. Review permissions and workload configurations along: pod:dev:foothold (Pod) → [runs_as] → sa:dev:webapp (ServiceAccount) → [can_list] → node:minikube (Node) → [can_get] → sa:default:webhook-patcher (ServiceAccount) → [can_patch] → webhook:test-pod-injector/pod-injector.webhook-test.svc (Webhook) → [can_mutate_workloads] → workload:kube-system:coredns (Workload)

edge.reason: host access: steal SA token from pod default/attacker-foothold on this node
mutating webhook "test-pod-injector/pod-injector.webhook-test.svc" intercepts pod creation — can inject sidecars, replace SA, modify security context

identity: → [can_exec] → pod:kube-system:kube-proxy-p6xvh (Pod) → [runs_as] → sa:kube-system:kube-proxy (ServiceAccount) → [granted_by] → crb:kubeadm:node-proxier (ClusterRoleBinding) → [bound_to] → clusterrole:system:node-proxier (ClusterRole) → [can_list] → node:minikube (Node) → [can_get] → sa:default:webhook-patcher (ServiceAccount) → [can_patch] → webhook:test-pod-injector/pod-injector.webhook-test.svc (Webhook) → [can_mutate_workloads] → workload:kube-system:kube-proxy (Workload)

Target: workload_takeover — Workload kube-system/kube-proxy: privileged containers: [kube-proxy]; hostNetwork=true; hostPath mounts: [/run/xtables.lock, /lib/modules]
evidence: identity: --(can_exec: SSAR confirmed: pods/exec create in namespace "kube-system")--> pod:kube-system:kube-proxy-p6xvh
pod:kube-system:kube-proxy-p6xvh --(runs_as: Pod runs as ServiceAccount)--> sa:kube-system:kube-proxy
sa:kube-system:kube-proxy --(granted_by: sa:kube-system:kube-proxy is subject of ClusterRoleBinding "kubeadm:node-proxier")--> crb:kubeadm:node-proxier
crb:kubeadm:node-proxier --(bound_to: ClusterRoleBinding → role)--> clusterrole:system:node-proxier
clusterrole:system:node-proxier --(can_list: clusterrole:system:node-proxier grants node access)--> node:minikube
node:minikube --(can_get: host access: steal SA token from pod default/attacker-foothold on this node)--> sa:default:webhook-patcher

sa:default:webhook-patcher --(can_patch: transitive: sa:default:webhook-patcher via crb:webhook-patcher-binding → clusterrole:webhook-patch-test)--> webhook:test-pod-injector/pod-injector.webhook-test.svc

webhook:test-pod-injector/pod-injector.webhook-test.svc --(can_mutate_workloads: mutating webhook "test-pod-injector/pod-injector.webhook-test.svc" intercepts pod creation — can inject sidecars, replace SA, modify security context)--> workload:kube-system:kube-proxy
risk_findings.mitigation: Break the attack path by removing at least one edge. Review permissions and workload configurations along: identity: → [can_exec] → pod:kube-system:kube-proxy-p6xvh (Pod) → [runs_as] → sa:kube-system:kube-proxy (ServiceAccount) → [granted_by] → crb:kubeadm:node-proxier (ClusterRoleBinding) → [bound_to] → clusterrole:system:node-proxier (ClusterRole) → [can_list] → node:minikube (Node) → [can_get] → sa:default:webhook-patcher (ServiceAccount) → [can_patch] → webhook:test-pod-injector/pod-injector.webhook-test.svc (Webhook) → [can_mutate_workloads] → workload:kube-system:kube-proxy (Workload)

edge.reason: SSAR confirmed: pods/exec create in namespace "kube-system"
host access: steal SA token from pod default/attacker-foothold on this node
mutating webhook "test-pod-injector/pod-injector.webhook-test.svc" intercepts pod creation — can inject sidecars, replace SA, modify security context
